data.title: BNDX: International Bond Diversification Doesn't Seem Worth It
data.description:
Are you wondering whether it's worth diversifying your U.S. Treasury portfolio? Well, if so, then you've come to the right place. This analysis covers the Vanguard Total International Bond Index Fund ETF Shares (NASDAQ:BNDX), a globally diversified bond vehicle with multi-sector exposure.

While we remain positive about U.S. treasuries, we acknowledge that some investors might seek external diversification, prompting us to communicate our thoughts of BNDX; here are a few things to consider.

Fund Overview

The Vanguard Total International Bond Index Fund ETF Shares tracks the Bloomberg Global Aggregate ex-USD Float Adjusted RIC Capped Index while hedging USD risk, essentially meaning investors gain exposure to major markets without facing USD risk exposure. Furthermore, the vehicle's effective duration is 7.1 years and the issuer diversified. Though its duration may change with time, BNDX is passively managed, meaning abrupt short-term shifts are unlikely.

The ETF's primary exposure is Japan, spanning over 12% of its portfolio. In addition, BNDX holds substantial exposure to France, Germany, Italy, the UK, Canada, and Spain—the diagram below illustrates BNDX's point-in-time exposure.

A critical note is BNDX's sector exposure. The vehicle doesn't isolate sovereign bonds. In fact, it invests throughout the credit hierarchy, possessing exposure to sovereign, corporate and asset-backed securities.

Here's a compact view of the ETF's credit exposure, supplementing the above-mentioned. Even though it emphasizes investment-grade credit (above BBB), BNDX doesn't isolate AAA-rated debt and invests in sub-grade debt.

The vehicle's historical performance echoes opportunity. BNDX has outperformed IEF ETF (IEF) in the past decade. Sure, IEF is an intermediate term U.S. Treasury pure play. Nevertheless, the comparison presents a telling relative view to those wondering about excess returns.

Aside: Risk premiums are mentioned later in the report.

Market Commentary

BNDX The Past Week

As many might know, U.S. Treasuries sold across the board last week. The event marked an unlikely outcome, as many anticipated treasuries to rise alongside fears of heightened recession risk. We remain upbeat about U.S. treasuries, yet think a relative comparison to BNDX communicates critical talking points. For example, BNDX holding up during a U.S. bond 'fire sale' suggests it might possess relative advantages in today's climate; is a relative advantage really in store? Let's find out.

Sovereign Spreads and CDS

Sovereign spreads, which measure the difference between a nation's 10-year yield versus the U.S. 10-year, often paint a valuable risk-adjusted return picture. According to our calculations, most of BNDX's key geographic areas have either faced spread tightening or additional weakening in recent years. Simultaneously, credit default swap spreads (agile measures of sovereign risk) have increased, suggesting higher absolute risk levels. In essence, quantitative measures indicate that most of BNDX's salient regions face receding risk-adjusted return prospects. The data suggests that opportunities remain in the U.K., but broad-based relative value still favours the United States.

Here's a view of the current environment. Please note the following: Swaps trade in a dark market, and intraday sovereign yields are volatile. As such, the numbers can differ slightly among vendors and analysts.

Country Sovereign Spread (Circa) 5-Y CDS (Circa)
Japan -3.01% 21.28 bps
France -0.93% 40.33 bps
Germany -1.51% 14.47 bps
UK 0.26% 22.09 bps
Canada -1.02% 39.60 bps
Italy -0.24% 61.67 bps

Source: Author's Calculations. Data: Trading Economics, World Government Bonds - Date: 07/04/2025 (Intraday)

This chart complements the argument by showing data from April 2022.

Country Sovereign Spread (Circa) 5-Y CDS (Circa)
Japan -1.58% 18.60 bps
France -1.41% 28.27 bps
Germany -1.75% 13.44 bps
UK -0.52% 12.76 bps
Canada 0.41% 32.34 bps
Italy 0.35% 100.14 bps

Source: Author's Calculations. Data: Trading Economics, World Government Bonds - Date: 04/04/2022 (Intraday)

Does qualitative data paint the full picture? No, let's examine qualitative events to extend the analysis.

Qualitative Tailwinds

  • Leading investment funds, including PIMCO have suggested that foreign diversification presents an opportunity in today's market environment. Institutional re-alignment can provide a qualitative base case to a pivot into foreign debt.
  • The U.K.'s economy is outperforming expectations in tandem with its positive sovereign spread. Collectively, these measures suggest an excess return opportunity has presented itself.

  • The EU recently announced intent to increase defence spending. Higher defence spending can rally interim GDP growth, which might be priced by the bond market in due course.
  • Germany has hinted at an attempt to become a capital importer under its new coalition government through the release of its debt brake and higher productivity spending.
  • The “basis trade” and "foreign adversaries selling U.S. treasuries" debacle is gathering steam. We think the narrative is overplayed, as re-location of U.S. debt investments from capital-exporting countries would be difficult to achieve. Moreover, a myriad of reasons could explain why treasuries sold aggressively in recent trading sessions; a basis trade argument is stale. Nonetheless, narrative often drives markets, and we think certain investors might re-locate their U.S. Treasury investments due to the prevailing sentiment of media and popular culture.

Qualitative Headwinds

  • France's enhanced sovereign spread should be placed into context. Fitch recently affirmed its AA- negative outlook, suggesting a spike in yields is warranted instead of presenting a value opportunity.
  • Germany's plans for fiscal reforms can be challenged by opposing parties. The country is in a political gridlock, presenting decision-making obstacles.
  • Much of Japan's economy relies on exports. Although tariff suspensions might provide interim benefits, a gradual decay of global trade confidence seems likely, paving the way for long-term headwinds.
  • The U.K.'s February GDP growth is an isolated event. The region has yet to deliver sustainable data to suggest it's exiting a trying economic period.

Credit Risk Outlook

Global corporate defaults experienced activity in 2023 and 2024, with speculative grade credit defaults rising. At the other end, investment-grade defaults remained relatively stable. While we expect deterioration in credit risk for the remainder of 2025, we think high-quality investment grade credits are safeguarded for now, unless severe tail risk surfaces on a global macro level (Think GFC, Covid, and not 2022/23).

Japan, which is BNDX's largest constituent, experienced docile re-ratings in asset-backed securities during 2024, likely mirrored in the corporate scene. In our view, the global trade war might pressure certain export companies, but we don't expect investment-grade entities to crumble just yet. Still, subprime debt might feel the brunt if deterioration in global economic sentiment resumes.

In essence, higher Probabilities of Default and Loss Given Default figures are possible for 2025. However, we see the phenomenon being non-investment grade-centric.

BNDX's Key Risk-Return Metrics

Yields

At the time of writing the article, BNDX had a 30-day SEC yield of 3.14%, which isn't particularly high, considering IEF ETF's 30-day SEC yield is 100 basis points higher and ZROZ's about 146 basis points. Although not pure comparisons, I'm merely trying to illustrate the differential by using familiar bond ETFs.

Risk

BNDX's return outlook seems tame. That said, a backward look at risk metrics implies superior risk protection. For instance, a glance at the BNDX ETF's risk profile may surprise many, as the ETF possesses a lower standard deviation and value-at-risk than the IEF ETF, which is astonishing considering the IEF ETF's "flight to safety" properties. Thus, the possibility arises: BNDX might be suitable for those with the primary goal of lowering risk while maintaining exposure to moderate returns.

Another Aside: A Monthly Value at risk (5%) measures the maximum loss an asset would experience for 95% of its trading months. It's backward-looking yet provides valuable inferences.

Conclusion

Our analysis concludes that BNDX ETF might be valuable to investors seeking risk-reduction while maintaining some risk exposure. The vehicle has illustrated a lower standard deviation than U.S. safe havens and often presented excess returns, implying that opportunity beckons, especially considering the ETF's diversification benefits.

Having said that, we maintain a high-conviction outlook on U.S. treasuries. We aren't crazy about EU, Canadian, and Japanese bonds given the additional compression of sovereign spreads. Moreover, severed CDS spreads and qualitative analysis suggest that BNDX's key regions are poorly aligned from a risk-return perspective.

In essence, we think BNDX could be a useful inclusion in some investors' portfolios, but we aren't committing to a 'Buy Rating', instead, we've decided to place BNDX on 'Hold.'

data.title: Top 5 ETFs In 2025 & Best International ETFs To Buy
data.description:
Sign up for Premium!

This video's transcript was generated by a third party. It is not curated or reviewed and is provided for convenience and information purposes only. The accuracy and completeness of the transcript are not guaranteed.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Content is offered for information purposes only. Unless stated otherwise, any and all individuals participating in the video are third parties that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body. Unless stated otherwise, the views or opinions expressed may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole.

The accuracy and completeness of content shared cannot be guaranteed. Seeking Alpha does not take account of your objectives or financial situation and does not offer any personalized investment advice. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker, U.S. investment adviser, or investment bank.

Daniel Snyder: Hey, everyone, Daniel Snyder. This is ETF Spotlight. Now, this week we're going to shake things up a little bit. We're going to look at the Top 5 ETFs so far this year based off of their inflows into the ETF. And so, we're going to jump through the Seeking Alpha website. We're going to look at the Quant rating system. And then we're also going to explore some of the country international ETFs and how you can use the ETF Screener here on the site to gain some exposure or put it on your watchlist if you're interested in those.

So, let's go ahead and dive on in. Now, first, I want to highlight, if you aren't aware, last year, we talked about this earlier in the ETF Spotlight season, that ETFs saw a record 2024 with inflows north of $1 trillion, and the money has not stopped just flowing into ETFs. Now, it could be for a variety of reasons. It could be, maybe somebody who doesn't feel comfortable picking individual stocks in this volatile market right now, and ETFs can be that safe space for people.

So, let's go ahead and highlight the Top 5. Coming in first place is VOO, the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF with that 3 basis point expense ratio. The Quant system is currently a Buy and the year-to-date flows have been $49 billion, which I don't know if you know this or not, but it has taken over the number one spot of assets under management, taking that throne away from the SPDR SPY ETF that everybody has known and loved for so long.

Now, coming in second place is IVV, the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF. Again, 3 basis point expense ratio. The Quant system is a Buy as well, and the year-to-date flows so far for this one have been $13.6 billion.

Now, coming in third place is SGOV, which is the iShares 0-3 Month Treasury Bond ETF that has an expense ratio of 9 basis points and the Quant system is a Buy rating on this ETF as well. I also do want to highlight if you haven't already, a friend of the show, John Bowman, there's his recent analysis on the SGOV. He has a Strong Buy on the stock, worth a read if you haven't done that already. So, and that has seen $13.3 billion of year-to-date flows so far.

Coming in fourth place is BIL, another taxable bond ETF here, the SPDR Bloomberg 1-3 Month T-Bill ETF. The expense ratio being 14 basis points on this one and the Quant system is also a Buy. And wrapping it up in fifth place is the VTI, Vanguard Total Stock Market Index Fund ETF with 3 basis point expense ratio. And the Quant system is actually a Hold on this one, probably because of that Momentum Factor Grade there that you see being a C+ down from the A-.

So, I want to take a quick second and highlight the ETF Screener side of our – oh, I forgot to mention, VTI year-to-date flows $11.2 billion. Now, I want to take a second and jump over to the Market Data. I want to show everybody something really fast. If you haven't already, you can come over here and you can click on Countries in the Market Data tab on the left-hand rail here, and you'll have a list of all the countries with ETFs representing those. And somebody might be looking for international exposure right now.

So, if I click on Year-To-Date returns here and filter by that, we can see that Poland, Spain, Greece, Austria, and Germany are here at the top of the list. And actually, if you scroll down, I want to highlight this as well. United States is here towards the bottom. But as well, Argentina has had a little bit of a fall from grace this year after such an amazing year last year.

So, I just want to highlight that real fast because now what we're going to do is, we're going to scroll down here on the left-hand nav bar, and we do have that Top ETFs screen that is updated every day and this is based off of the Quant rating system. So, if you always want to keep an eye on that, that's right there, but I want to highlight the ETF Screener section.

So, we have a list of preset screeners here. And you'll notice that one of them is the Top International Equity ETFs. So, we're going to go ahead and click on that. And I want to show you that, here at the top of the list, Argentina is still ranking high, but Spain, Greece, Poland, Germany, you have options of ETFs that are rated highly by the Quant rating system right here already for you.

And the easiest thing to do is, you can add Advanced Filters, you can look at Dividends, you can look at their Holdings, say, I want to see the number of holdings in them. I want to see their performance year-to-date, which is what we were talking about and see risk or anything else, but I'm going to go ahead and click Done on that. You'll see that instantly pops up here on the right-hand side, giving me the data that I want to keep an eye on.

Now, what you can also do here, which is really great, is you can either save this screen by just clicking Save As and click that and then you'll have it under your Saved Screen section here on Seeking Alpha, or you can actually create a portfolio or a watchlist, if you will, to keep an eye on them in the future.

So, for example, I don't want to watch Argentina. It’s not probably doing that performance that I'm seeing so far this year, but I can keep an eye on Spain, Greece, Poland and Germany as well and just go ahead and throw that into a new portfolio here. And I can also have the portfolio updates into my email every single morning about what's going on within these ETFs. I can dive deeper into these ETFs if I want to explore them one by one.

I can come in, I can look at the seasonality of the ETF, I can look at the holdings. If they pay a dividend, I'll see that as well. So, a lot of cool functionality here on Seeking Alpha. So, I just wanted to highlight that. Hope everybody has a great week. Thank you for tuning into this episode and we'll see you again here soon on ETF Spotlight.

Sign up for Premium!

data.title: PayPal: Recent Weakness Looks Like A Golden Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Investment thesis

Despite a strong November-December rally, my previous bullish thesis about PayPal (NASDAQ:PYPL) did not age well, as the stock is tanking in 2025. As a result, PYPL is currently almost 20% cheaper than it was on November 3. I think that this weakness is temporary because PayPal's management confidently executes on the company's growth strategy and secular trends are favorable for the entire industry. Furthermore, the stock is extremely attractively valued after the YTD dip. All in all, I reiterate my “Strong Buy” rating for PYPL.

Recent developments

PayPal shared its latest quarterly earnings on February 4, delivering a confident double beat against consensus estimates. Despite the uncertain environment and inflation still running above the Fed's targets, PYPL delivered a 4.2% YoY revenue growth. Moreover, there was a relatively wide positive adjusted EPS surprise.

There was the adjusted EPS contraction in Q4 when compared to YoY. However, it was not due to some operating issues. The unfavorable difference took place as there was a one-off $275 million non-operating loss during the quarter, compared to a non-operating gain of $113 million during Q4 2023. From the operating leverage perspective, performance was solid. The gross margin expanded YoY from 39.96% to 41.64%.

Solid operating leverage helped the company generate $1.1 billion in free cash flow [FCF] during Q4 2024. Strong FCF performance ensured improvement in the company's financial position. PayPal's financial position is robust, with the cash balance just around 10% lower compared to the total debt. In addition, there was a $1.3 billion buyback during the quarter, which reinforces the management's confidence in PayPal's future prospects.

Apart from PayPal's strong financial performance and position, there is another large positive factor. Industry trends remain very favorable for PayPal as the digital payment market is thriving. According to Precedence Research, the market is expected to deliver a robust 17% CAGR over the next decade. I think that this is a massive bullish catalyst because PYPL has been historically strong in capitalizing on secular tailwinds. Of course, there is intensifying competition in the industry as giants like Apple, Google, and Meta are expanding their footprints. Nevertheless, I think that a 17% industry CAGR will give opportunity for several players to capitalize on industry growth.

As a company that is highly committed to delivering a customer-friendly and safe experience of conducting digital payments, I think that PayPal is well-positioned to absorb positive industry trends despite competition. During the latest Morgan Stanley Technology, Media & Telecom Conference, PayPal's management highlighted the company's firm commitment to delivering on its growth strategy. Branded checkout remains the company's main priority and there is a clear roadmap to achieve 80% global adoption of PayPal's enhanced checkout experience by 2027.

Venmo, the company's fastest-growing business of the last couple of years, is set to unlock its full monetization potential. This will be achieved by integrating Venmo into the company's broader ecosystem.

The management also reiterated their firm commitment to innovate. PayPal is investing heavily in AI to streamline its internal processes, which is also highly likely to help in creating value for shareholders and enhance customers' experiences. From the innovation perspective, the company is also leveraging data with commerce API. It is crucial as it enables merchants to personalize the shopping experience by offering customized interfaces based on shopper profiles. The management expects it to become a major differentiator in driving customer conversion and loyalty.

Last but not least, the management is also working hard on leveraging new partnerships, which will help to expand PayPal's reach. The company recently added Solana and Chainlink cryptocurrencies to its platforms. Apart from the improved reach, this move also improves the experience for customers by providing greater flexibility and choice. According to Slickcharts, Solana and Chainlink are among the top-15 largest cryptocurrencies, with a combined market cap of around $75 billion.

Valuation update

PYPL experienced a quite sharp decline in 2025 with a 27% YTD share price decrease. The valuation ratios look quite good, especially compared to PYPL's historical averages. In addition, most of the ratios are lower compared to the sector median as well. In my opinion, the biggest green flag is a low 7.8 forward Price/Cash flow ratio, which is substantially lower than both the sector median and historical average.

As usual, I started my DCF analysis with the cost of equity calculation. This one is figured out quite easily as the CAPM formula is straightforward, and all the necessary variables are easily available on the Internet. The cost of equity is 10.5%.

Since PayPal has a notable portion of debt in its capital structure, WACC will be a more suitable choice to discount the DCF model. The cost of debt was figured out using the company's TTM interest expense divided by the current outstanding total debt. As a result, the discount rate is 9.32%.

Revenue assumption for FY2026 is $35.14 billion. I think that the mid-single digit long-term revenue growth rate projected by consensus is too conservative given the projected double-digit industry growth, which I have highlighted above. Therefore, for the base-case scenario, I apply a 10% revenue CAGR.

To be conservative, I use the FCF margin adjusted for stock-based compensation [SBC]. For PYPL, the TTM FCF ex-SBC margin is 7.2%. Due to intense competition, I applied a subtle 25-bps yearly expansion of the FCF margin. The perpetual growth rate is quite conservative at 3%.

The base-case scenario suggests that the business's fair value is around $103 billion. This is 66% higher than the current market cap, indicating a massive upside potential. The margin of safety is huge because even applying a 2% perpetual growth rate combined with a 5% revenue CAGR suggests that the stock is 14% undervalued.

Risks update

We navigate an extremely uncertain environment, which weighs on the sentiment around the entire stock market. New large tariffs were introduced and then suddenly paused. I think that such a high level of uncertainty might make investors much more defensive, leading to capital rotating from growth to value. As a result, growth stocks like PYPL might suffer despite being high-quality companies.

Apart from the sentiment, there is a great level of uncertainty about the long-term effect of tariffs on the U.S. economy. Several large investment banks increased their recession probability projections. This looks like a fair move because tariffs can potentially lead to undermining the intensity of international trade. If that is the case, PayPal might suffer, as there will be less economic activity and fewer digital payment transactions overall.

In addition, PYPL has been historically quite weak in May. According to the above bar chart, the win rate in May was only 33% over the last decade. On the other hand, PYPL's performance during summer is usually robust. Therefore, investors might be better off by leaving some room to double down in case of share weakness in May.

Bottom line

To conclude, despite the uncertainty caused by tariffs, I think that PYPL remains a “Strong Buy”. The company continues executing on its growth strategy, secular trends appear to be favorable, and the stock is extremely undervalued.

data.title: Tariffs Keep The Federal Reserve Focused On Inflation, But No Change Is Expected For Interest Rates
data.description:
By James Picerno

Judging by the recent decline in the policy-sensitive US 2-year Treasury yield and Fed funds futures, market expectations continue to anticipate a rate cut, if not at next month’s policy meeting then by June. TMC Research’s Fed Funds Model also suggests that easing monetary policy is appropriate, based on current conditions. But Federal Reserve officials continue to downplay expectations that a rate cut is near. A key factor: tariffs could elevate prices.

Federal Reserve governor Chris Waller is the latest in a series of policy officials who are talking up the goal of keeping inflation stable. That task could become more challenging in the near term due to tariffs, he explained on Monday. Higher inflation implies rate hikes. But Waller also said that he expects any inflation increase triggered by tariffs will be temporary due to the countereffects of slower growth and higher unemployment.

Under the large tariff scenario, economic growth is likely to slow to a crawl and significantly raise the unemployment rate. I do expect inflation to rise significantly, but if inflation expectations remain well anchored, I also expect inflation to return to a more moderate level in 2026. Inflation could rise starting in a few months and then move back down toward our target possibly as early as by the end of this year.

He added that it’s not yet clear that rate hikes will be necessary.

The preemptive policy cuts we did last fall can allow us some time to wait and see if the hard data catch up to the soft data or vice versa and how much of the tariff will be passed through to the consumer. In such a scenario, the outlook for monetary policy might not look much different than it did before March 1.

Two recent surveys of inflation expectations suggest that consumers are expecting prices to rise, which can be a self-fulling prophecy. Notably, the initial April estimate of inflation expectations in the University of Michigan’s widely followed poll surged to 6.7%. By contrast, the New York Fed’s consumer survey is substantially lower at 3.6% for the year-ahead outlook, albeit as of March. Both numbers are well above the reported 2.6% consumer inflation year-over-year trend through March, which in turn is moderately above the Fed’s 2% inflation target.

Waller noted that he places more trust in the Treasury market’s implied inflation forecast for expectations. The 5-year breakeven inflation rate, based on the nominal 5-year yield less its 5-year counterpart, was 2.31% on Monday (Apr. 15), which is roughly in line with the pre-tariff turmoil.

TMC Research’s Fed funds model shows a widening spread between the current median Fed funds target rate and the falling estimate of a neutral rate - a sign that Fed policy may be passively shifting to a more restrictive phase lately, albeit on the margins so far.

What could change the calculus and move Waller (and presumably the Fed) to consider rate hikes? Stronger growth in the labor market could be a trigger.

Meantime, the Fed appears to be watching and waiting for more data and allowing the current policy stance to continue for the near term. The wild card is still tariffs, and these are still early days. For now, the central bank seems to be comfortable with maintaining its current 4.25-4.50% Fed funds target range until inflation and/or the labor market data provides a compelling reason to change tack.

Original Source: The Milwaukee Company

data.title: Tariffs Increase To 245% On China: What's The Limit?
data.description:
When tariffs on China were around 50%, I thought they were already very high and I never expected that after a few weeks they would reach 245%. It's a huge figure, and just as it has done so far, China will probably respond with a similar tariff. But how far will the world's two leading powers go? Increasing tariffs by 100% is no longer a surprise, in fact, the S&P 500 futures (SPX) are down “only” -0.80%.

The position of the United States and that of China

The trade war began when President Trump announced that he would impose tariffs on his main trading partners because they were responsible for the huge trade deficit. At that stage, the stock market fell by double digits in a short period of time, only to partially recover when the tariffs were suspended for 90 days and reduced to 10% in the meantime. China was the only country that did not receive a suspension or even a temporary reduction of tariffs. On the contrary, a tit-for-tat dispute began and has now culminated in the current 245%.

Being the only country that can really stand up to the United States, China did not give in to this aggressive attitude and responded appropriately. However, as stated by Foreign Ministry spokesman Lin Jian, China doesn't want any of this:

China is not willing to fight (such a war), nor is it afraid of fighting. If the US genuinely wants to solve the problem through dialogue and negotiation, it should give up its approach of imposing extreme pressure, stop threatening and blackmailing, and engage in dialogue with the Chinese side on the basis of equality, respect and mutual benefit.

The end of the trade war depends solely on the United States, but if tariffs continue to increase by triple digits, then China will do the same. In my opinion, its behavior cannot be blamed; after all, it is the second economic power and must demonstrate a certain resilience. In fact, it is showing it: in Q1 2025, GDP grew by 5.40%, well above the 5.10% target. Logically, there were no import tariffs of 245% during this period, but Sheng Laiyun, Deputy Commissioner of the NBS of China, does not believe they can seriously impact the country's long-term growth:

China’s economic foundation is stable, resilient and has great potential, so we have the courage, ability and confidence to cope with external challenges and achieve the established development goals.

Furthermore, a process of diversification is underway in terms of exports:

A diversified market pattern is taking shape, which also means that our dependence on a single export market in a certain country is declining. Therefore, China’s foreign trade is relatively resilient. The just-released first-quarter foreign trade data also shows that despite the increase in external restrictions, exports still grew by 6.9%.

In short, tariffs of 245% certainly won't favor the country's economic growth, but China's future doesn't seem compromised at all. In light of these considerations, I'm reminded of Victor Gao's words a few days ago:

China is fully prepared to fight to the very end because the world is big enough that the United States is not the totality of the market in the world. So if the United States wants to go in that direction of completely shutting itself out of the China market, be my guest. We don’t care. China has been here for 5,000 years. Most of the time, there was no United States and we survived. And if the United States wants to bully China, we will deal with the situation without the United States, and we expect to survive for another 5,000 years.

It would seem that the United States has chosen to antagonize the wrong opponent, and China is willing to go all the way. Among other things, the different monetary policies to which the two countries are subjected could be a further point in China's favor. The United States is still struggling with sticky inflation and interest rates are still high, while China is in the midst of an expansionary fiscal and monetary policy. To recover from the real estate crisis, it is injecting liquidity into the system and will continue to do so over the next quarters. Tariffs will certainly be a burden, but in an economic context with low rates, the growth of local companies will be favored. This effect, combined with export diversification, could partially offset the enormous burden of U.S. tariffs.

But a trade war isn't just about import tariffs, it can evolve into something more devious, such as knocking out the best companies in the respective countries. We have already reached this stage: China has restricted imports of Boeing aircraft, and the United States has restricted exports of the most powerful Nvidia chips to China. These types of maneuvers are very dangerous for investors, as the affected stocks can quickly become falling knives. We don't know which companies will be the next to suffer the repercussions of the trade war, but I wouldn't be surprised if it were Apple and Tesla, two companies that depend on China for manufacturing and sales. We'll see, but from this point of view China still has an advantage. Preventing Chinese tech giants from selling in the U.S. wouldn't affect their finances too much, as they mainly sell to the east, but the situation is different in reverse.

No one wins in trade wars, so it's best to end them as soon as possible. In any case, in this U.S.-China confrontation, I believe that the latter has an advantage and can afford to stand up to the aggressive increase in tariffs. Whether it's 245%, 500% or 1000%, China will continue to fight back because it has the leverage to do so; on the other hand, I'm not sure the U.S. can really sustain this situation for more than a few quarters.

Actually, I believe that if China wanted to, it could end this war in a relatively short time, as it has the greatest advantage of all on its side: a virtual monopoly on rare earths. By preventing exports to the U.S., it would lose a lot of money, but it would completely inhibit American economic growth; no one would benefit, but China would probably come out less weakened. Rare earths are the basis of all major technological devices, renewable energy, weapons and defense. Not having them means giving up all this and not being able to continue exploiting the current technological revolution.

The United States may have trillions of dollars to spend on AI CapEx, but without rare earths elements (REEs) they are going nowhere, and China accounts for 99% of the world's heavy REEs processing. It's a bit like knowing how to make the best pizza in the world but not having the flour.

Among China's latest responses, in addition to raising tariffs, the Chinese Ministry of Commerce has imposed export restrictions on 7 out of 17 rare earth elements (REEs) to the U.S. This is a clear signal that China is not afraid to use this leverage and is willing to go further in the event of a further increase in tariffs. At the moment no country in the world can replace China in the processing of REEs (and none is close to doing so), so in my opinion the U.S. must act cautiously from now on.

To go back to the title, so what is the limit of this trade war? In my opinion it lies in China's willingness to further reduce REEs exports to the U.S. It is at that point that perhaps the tones will be more diplomatic.

data.title: Verve Therapeutics: FDA Fast Track And Eli Lilly Partnership Upgrade It To A 'Buy'
data.description:
Verve Therapeutics (NASDAQ:VERV) basically specializes in one-time gene-editing treatments for cardiovascular conditions. Their pipeline includes VERVE-102, VERVE-201, in Phase 1, and VERVE-301 in the pre-clinical stage. In particular, VERVE-102 is a treatment for high LDL cholesterol that recently received the FDA’s Fast Track designation due to its early favorable safety and efficacy results. As for VERVE-201, this is intended for homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia [HoFH] and refractory hypercholesterolemia. But interestingly, I now believe VERV has much more tangible evidence for its science compared to the first time I reviewed the stock. All while also trading at a cheaper price than when I initially covered it. Hence, I consider it a good time to lean bullish on the shares.

Modern Gene Editing Cuts

Verve Therapeutics is currently based in Cambridge, Massachusetts, and it’s a clinical-stage biotech. Broadly speaking, VERV’s main focus is on creating single-dose gene-editing therapies that can treat cardiovascular diseases. They were founded back in 2018 and use two technologies to edit DNA called 1) base editing and 2) novel editing. Base editing essentially changes the DNA’s fundamental nitrogenous bases of A to G or C to T without cutting or double-stranded breaks. This base editing feature makes base editing suitable for silencing genes such as PCSK9.

However, novel editing generates editing cuts using engineered nucleases, but it’s much more experimental. This is part of VERVE-301, which VERV nominated as a potential Development Candidate in its partnership with Eli Lilly (LLY). Still, it could become suitable for other, more complex targets like the LPA gene. And since my previous June 2024 Hold rating on VERV, they’ve continued making interesting progress with VERVE-102. Concretely, we now have its early data, for which the FDA gave it a Fast Track designation. It also triggered a milestone payment for its partnership with LLY.

In that sense, it’s worth mentioning that VERV prioritized VERVE-102 after VERVE-101 was paused. Unfortunately, VERVE-101 seems to have run into safety issues due to its lipid nanoparticle [LNP], which the candidate used as a delivery system. In contrast, VERVE-102 uses a different LNP delivery system that incorporates GalNAc. This is a sugar molecule that can target specifically liver cells, which makes it ideal for treating heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia [HeFH] and atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease [ASCVD].

Also, VERVE-102's GalNAc-LNP combination delivers VERVE-102’s gene-editing payload directly into the liver and silences genes like PCSK9. Since PCSK9 is closely related to high LDL cholesterol levels, this is what ultimately yields VERVE-102’s therapeutic effects. Moreover, at the time of this writing, VERVE-102 is currently in Phase 1b, focusing on evaluating its safety and efficacy for LDL-C reduction. Plus, it has had its FDA designation since April 2025, specifically for hyperlipidemia patients with high cardiovascular risk.

Aside from VERVE-102, the company has VERVE-201. But this IP is actually for homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia [HoFH] and refractory hypercholesterolemia, and approximately 13% of ASCVD patients who are refractory suffer from it. Also, similarly to VERVE-102, VERVE-201 is also initiating its Phase 1b trials. Lastly, VERV’s VERVE-301 is a candidate indicated for ASCVD, but with high Lp(a) patients.

This Lp(a) factor is often an inherited lipid particle that increases the risk for ASCVD, which affects approximately 20%-25% of the world’s global population. Having said that, it’s important to remember that one of VERV’s main value drivers is its partnership with LLY. This collaboration has been in place since 2023, and it’s focused on developing gene-editing targets for Lp(a) using VERVE-301. And more recently, in January 2025, VERV announced VERVE-301 as its candidate for the LPA gene, which aims to reduce Lp(a) levels.

After that, by February 2025, this triggered VERV to become eligible for a milestone payment from LLY as part of this agreement. But this partnership goes beyond just that, including up to $465 million in milestone payments plus tiered royalties. It’s also a relatively favorable deal for VERV, as they’re only responsible for completing Phase 1 trials with this IP. Beyond that regulatory milestone, LLY will oversee further R&D trials, all the way up to its potential commercialization.

VERVE-102 Early Human Results

But probably one of its main catalysts recently for the stock happened shortly after VERV announced positive early results from its Phase 1b trial on VERVE-102. In fact, VERV stock rallied roughly 23% on this news, which is reasonable since I think it’s easily VERV’s main value driver right now. In any event, this trial showed VERVE-102 indeed has a viable safety and efficacy profile, which even exceeded expectations.

In particular, it appears that VERVE-102’s most impressive results for reducing LDL-C by up to 69% happened at the 0.6 mg/Kg dose. The only potential drawback from this trial is that they enrolled very few patients, which is why this high-dose cohort only used n=3. So, I wouldn’t say those results have much statistical power for now, but when you look at the trial as a whole, there’s a clear trend that suggests an effective dose-dependent therapeutic effect.

That’s why VERV is now planning on initiating Phase 2 trials on VERVE-102 in the second semester of 2025. Additionally, it’s possible that LLY might “opt in” at this R&D stage. LLY initially acquired those opt-in rights from Beam Therapeutics (BEAM) back in 2023 when they decided to offload this IP. And if LLY decides to go forward and exercise its right, it would have to pay a third of VERVE-102’s R&D costs in exchange for sharing its commercialization profits in the US.

Note that VERV would still hold all of VERVE-102’s rights in other jurisdictions. So, whether LLY opts in or not remains to be seen, but I think it should be a positive catalyst for the stock if they decide to go forward with this. Note that we should know LLY’s decision on this by the second half of this year. After all, it would not only provide some financial support for VERV but it would also act as a validating factor for VERVE-102’s true potential.

Likewise, Heart-2’s (its Phase 1b trial’s name) results also suggest that VERVE-102 could be an alternative to lifelong statin use or PCSK9 inhibitors. Currently, Amgen Inc. (AMGN)’s Repatha is one of the most popular treatments for inhibiting PCSK9. AMGN’s Repatha is basically a monoclonal antibody administered by subcutaneous injection, every 2 weeks or monthly. But unfortunately, this is a lifelong treatment as it has to be periodically dosed into patients to maintain healthy LDL-C levels.

This is why VERVE-102 could potentially be a “one-and-done” treatment for this particular condition. It would just silence the PCSK9 gene in liver cells and permanently fix patients’ LDL-C issues. Naturally, this type of gene-editing treatment will probably have a much higher upfront cost. But, in the long run, it could still be much more affordable than lifelong repetitive Repatha injections.

And remember, HeFH is a potentially huge TAM for VERV as it’s projected to reach up to $32.9 billion by 2034. Obviously, since VERVE-102 would be an expensive gene-editing therapy, I doubt it will immediately gain a substantial market share here. But nonetheless, it shows there’s a lot of room for growth in just this particular indication (contingent on FDA approval, which isn’t guaranteed).

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, VERV currently trades at a $441.3 million market cap. This is after roughly 67% off its recent April 2025 lows. But interestingly, it’s still down 42% from the February 2025 highs, so there could still be more room to run even at these levels.

And if we look at its balance sheet, VERV now holds approximately $524.3 million in cash against no debt (other than regular operating liabilities). Its book value also stands at $493.4 million, indicating a cheap P/B of just 0.9. For context, their peers’ median P/B is much higher at 1.9, which is partly why I feel this rally could still have more gas left in the tank, so to speak.

Naturally, while its valuation seems cheap, we can’t forget that VERVE-102 still remains years away from any FDA approvals. Of course, the upside could be massive if they eventually develop this gene therapy for ASCVD. But still, VERV remains a very long-term commitment, which does increase its risk profile relative to other biotechs with late-stage IPs. And in the meantime, VERV will continue burning through its cash reserves. In fact, as it progresses into late-stage trials, it’s very likely its cash burn will increase. This is why the company itself believes its runway lasts until approximately mid-2027.

In any event, I estimate their quarterly cash burn reached $42.2 million in Q4 2024. I got that figure by simply adding their cash flow from operations and CAPEX, which suggests a runway of approximately 12.4 quarters. But more or less 1 quarter has passed since then, so its runway is probably closer to 11.4 quarters, or 2.9 years. So management’s runway figure seems reasonable. But it also means, even in the best-case scenario, most of its liquid resources will probably be spent by the time VERVE-102 is close to a regulatory submission.

So this will definitely gradually chip away at shareholders, which could very well keep a cap on its upside potential for the foreseeable future. And also, if LLY decides to opt in, it could further extend its runway and de-risk VERV even more. Thus, given their recent progress with VERVE-102 and VERV’s very cheap valuation multiples, I feel it could still make sense as a long-term speculative “Buy” at these levels.

Conclusion: “Buy” Upgrade

Overall, I feel VERV has now provided much more concrete trial data for its main value driver, VERVE-102. And interestingly, it’s actually even 11.1% cheaper than the first time I covered it and rated it a “Hold.” So I believe its risk-reward profile is now much more favorable, even though it still remains a long-term speculative proposition. This is why I think it’s fair to upgrade it to a “Buy” rating, but make sure you size your position on VERV carefully. I wouldn’t say it’s worth being a major portion of a biotech portfolio just yet, but it does make sense as part of a basket of moonshot biotech plays.

data.title: GXO Logistics: I Was Wrong, Not A Buy
data.description:
In November 2023, I covered GXO Logistics (NYSE:GXO) and downgraded the stock from strong buy to buy. However, the stock has declined nearly 40% since then. In this report, I will be revisiting the stock to assess whether it is still a buy or whether the outlook for the company has deteriorated.

GXO Logistics Faces A Challenging Landscape

Being active in the field of providing logistics solutions is not easy despite the fact that this is an industry with huge value. Required investments are high and the market is highly fragmented, leading to fierce competition that may result in thin margins. Many companies are also looking to grow through mergers and acquisitions, further amplifying the cash-intensive nature of the industry. Furthermore, the industry is susceptible to the effects of macroeconomic weakening and erosion of consumer confidence and that's definitely the case now.

When I covered GXO Logistics in 2023, it had adjusted its organic growth guidance for 2023 downward. For 2024, the company had guided for 2% to 5% in organic growth. The company ended up delivering 3% organic growth. So, it's not performing poorly at all.

However, the company’s stock price have been challenged. There have been weak spots in Europe that could possibly justify share price pressure, but generally we have seen the stock underperform peers while it was not delivering abysmal results. What did not help the company’s share price is that the company turned down an acquisition offer. Generally, we see that companies in the logistics space are very willing to be acquired or merge operations. So, the rejection to be acquired went against what I would call "the industry trend." Furthermore, its $1 billion acquisition of Wincanton received pushback from competitors and watchdogs. The acquisition of Wincanton was eventually completed in August 2024, but the acquisition is still being probed, and until that probe is concluded, deeper integration of Wincanton into GXO Logistics will be delayed.

The company’s CEO, Malcolm Wilson, is also set to retire in 2025 which may also not help investor sentiment.

So, a lot happened since the last time I covered the stock.

GXO Net Income Falls On Integration And Litigation Costs

A quick look at the results in 2024 showed that the company booked 3% organic growth and 20% total sales growth. Net income, however, declined from $233 million to $138 million. This was primarily due to transaction costs, litigation expenses and an increase in SGA driven by the acquisition of Wincanton. Furthermore, gross margins declined from17.8% to 15.9%.

Adjusted EBITDA of $815 million exceeded the FY2024 guidance of $760-$790 million while free cash flow conversion of 31% came in at the lower end of the guided range.

GXO Logistics Outlook For 2025 Is Soft

For 2025, the company is guiding for 4.2% growth in EBITDA at the mid point supported by 3% to 6% organic revenue growth and EPS of $2.40-$2.60. Analysts now estimate EPS for FY25 at $2.44, down from estimates of $3.17 as GXO will face realignment headwinds in the first quarter. Furthermore, the macroeconomic uncertainty may exert pressure on the business as well.

On Feb. 16, GXO announced a share repurchase authorization of $500 million. There are two ways to look at this announcement. The first way is that company may deem the current stock price too low, providing an attractive opportunity to buy back shares. The other one is that as the company may see limited space for M&A, it returns value to shareholders instead. That would ultimately indicate that the business has to lean more and more on its organic growth and that organic growth is not huge and very susceptible to macroeconomics.

GXO is the largest pure-play contract logistics provider in the world and it could be that with the objections against the acquisition of Wincanton, the company did realize that due to its current scale it may receive more and more pushback from regulators to grow inorganically.

GXO Logistics Stock Is A Hold

To determine multi-year price targets The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

While I previously have been upbeat about the prospects of GXO Logistics, I'm now downgrading the stock to hold. The company is acquiring businesses, but what we do see is that its operating cash flow did not improve year-on-year. So, from an OCF perspective, market pressures are for the time being offset by the positive impact of acquisitions. The company did authorize $500 million in stock repurchases, which I have modeled in. However, given that the company will have to borrow to fund the share repurchases and the fact that the stock is fairly valued with earnings for FY26 in mind I do not see it as the most prudent way of deploying cash.

EBITDA is expected to grow at a rate of 7% annually with free cash flow growth expected to grow 13.4% annually. However, the stock is already richly valued compared to peers and with no upside against FY26 estimates. Against FY27 estimates, there's 28% growth. Given that this is more than 12% months away with no upside within that timeframe, I'm assigning a hold rating.

Conclusion: GXO Logistics Is Not Extremely Attractive

I do like the business model that GXO Logistics has. However, organic growth is not extremely strong and the macroeconomic landscape is becoming more and more challenging. Given that the company is not quite adding through its operating cash flow from acquisitions or organic growth, I'm less upbeat about the company now than I was in 2023 and assign a hold rating noting potential upside for 2027.

data.title: Solid March U.S. Retail Sales
data.description:
Retail sales were up by 1.4 percent in March, helped by motor vehicle sales but held back by sales at gasoline stations (on lower gasoline prices). There were upward revisions to February, but the retail sales control group (removing autos, building supplies, and gasoline stations) was up by only 0.4 percent (a bit less than expected).

While there may have been some buying-ahead behavior (especially autos), the retail sales figures were solid and indicated that the consumer continued to enjoy the great American sport of shopping. This continues to be supported by still strong job and income growth, with the weekly unemployment claims not yet showing any meaningful weakness.

This report – other than for buying ahead – would not have had major tariff impacts, but that will likely change as soon as April. But because retail sales are reported in nominal terms, higher prices can offset (at least some of) a slowdown in real spending activity.At least through March, the underlying U.S. economy (C + I) continued to move ahead at an above-trend pace.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Outset Medical: The Worst Is Over, But Execution Risk Remains High
data.description:
Investment thesis

Outset Medical, Inc. (NASDAQ:OM) offers a less complex solution for dialysis at a lower cost, targeting the home, acute and post-acute markets which together represent a TAM of over $11.4 billion. The market for dialysis in the US is forecasted to grow at a 6.1% CAGR until 2030, providing a strong tailwind for demand. Unfortunately, the company faced regulatory challenges last year which led to full year revenue falling 13%. This headwind contributed to slower commercial success, which has clouded the company's growth story.

However, Outset Medical has entered this year on a solid financial foundation with a strong order backlog, which makes its outlook appear promising. While I see a realistic path to profitability and potential upside in the stock from current levels, there are several risks that make me hesitant and warrant a more cautious approach. I prefer waiting on the sidelines until I see clear evidence of execution by management in returning the business to low-teens growth coupled with reduced cash burn.

Strengthened balance sheet supports a more promising outlook

The company significantly improved its financial position at the beginning of this year by receiving private placement funding amounting to $169 million. The capital injection however significantly diluted its existing shareholders, thereby requiring the company to perform a 1 for 15 reverse stock split. Following this financing, the company had $210 million in cash and $100 million in debt, with the debt maturity extended until 2030.

With the FDA issues now clearly sorted out according to the press release in February, the company is now well set to tackle the large market opportunity ahead of it. Q4 results came in above management's guidance and 2025 revenue guidance calls for growth to accelerate to between 2% to 10%. The sales pipeline appears to be gathering momentum, with its CEO stating on the Q4 earnings call that:

Just in Q4 alone, we saw more than 10% growth in the 2025 pipeline. Now roughly 70% of our top opportunities forecasted to close this year are already in the latest stages of our sales process. That is an advantage we have never enjoyed in the past as we've entered a new year.

With the company generating significant losses and burning through large amounts of cash each year, accelerating revenue growth and lifting gross margins are two crucial drivers behind the company's path to profitability. In my previous write-up, I covered the factors that will contribute to higher gross margins. For this year, I expect gross margins to approach 40%, which at the midpoint of revenue guidance of $120 million, implies $48 million in gross profit. With annual operating expenses targeted to be around $90 million, and net interest expenses of close to $5 million, the company is likely to report a loss of approximately $47 million in 2025. Given that inventory levels are fairly elevated, I expect cash burn to be slightly lesser than this figure, as inventory levels gradually come down during the year.

Looking ahead, I see this business being capable of growing at low-teens rates in 2026 and beyond. With management aiming for gross margins to reach 50% in the medium term, I see a path where the company reaches breakeven EBITDA in 2028 on a revenue base of $175 million, with GAAP profitability likely in the following year.

I see cash burn in 2026 reducing to approximately $38 million and then coming down in the following years as revenue increases and gross margins approaching 50%, with only modest operating expense growth. According to my estimates, the total cash burn from 2025 to 2028 should be around $130 million, leaving a healthy margin for the current cash level of $210 million. While the cash runaway appears sufficient, the management team has a tough challenge ahead to prudently balance revenue growth with keeping expenses under check, in order to remain on track to be able to generate positive FCF in 2029. This is imperative to ease investor concerns with regard to its debt that comes due in 2030.

My approach to valuing OM stock

Let's take a look at a relevant peer like DaVita Inc. (DVA), which has gross margins in the low 30s, yet impressive net margins of over 15%, driven by its massive scale. While Outset Medical aims for gross margins to approach 50% in the medium term, due to its much smaller scale, I think it is reasonable to expect that it could end up at similar steady-state net margins as DaVita.

Assuming this is the case, if we apply a revenue multiple of 2, which is the revenue multiple that DVA stock trades for, this would imply a market cap for Outset Medical of $240 million. This translates to a stock price of $13.6, implying an upside potential of close to 25%. While it can be argued that OM stock deserves to trade at a premium due to its higher growth rate compared to DVA which is growing at only mid-single digit rates, I would point out that a discount is warranted due to its significantly higher risk profile. Since I am forecasting significant cash burn in the coming quarters, I have not factored the net cash position into my valuation.

Now let's approach valuation on a Price to Gross Profit basis, and assign a gross profit multiple of 8 to OM stock which is where fast-growing MedTech companies such as TransMedics Group, Inc. (TMDX), DexCom, Inc. (DXCM) and iRhythm Technologies, Inc. (IRTC) trade at. This translates to a valuation of over $400 million, or $21.3 per share, based on $48 million in gross profit expected for 2025. While this represents an attractive upside of nearly 100%, a lot of things need to go right for the company to accelerate and consistently maintain revenue growth in the mid-teens like these MedTech peers.

Currently, my confidence level is quite low when it comes to execution by management, especially given the recent history, which together with some additional risks I see, makes me hesitant towards becoming more positive on the stock. It is noteworthy that the company remains an attractive acquisition candidate considering its size relative to its market opportunity coupled with its superior product offering.

Risks

While I have already walked through my concerns related to management's execution when it comes to prolonging the cash runway and handling its debt, there are a couple of additional risks as well for investors to consider. Given that the manufacturing of its units is done in Mexico, the prolonging of the ongoing tariffs or future tariffs on goods from Mexico could significantly impact the company's gross margin outlook and path to profitability.

While there are no direct competitors, commercialization has so far been quite challenging and still remains to be proven in the coming quarters, now that the regulatory issues appear to be behind them.

Conclusion

2025 is a crucial year for Outset Medical with management looking to turnaround the business and return it to solid organic growth with improving margins. With a strong financial position and a realistic path to achieve profitability beyond 2028, I acknowledge that there is an attractive potential upside for investors if things work out well. However, I am opting to stick to the sidelines until I gain further confidence in the execution by the management team. As a result, I am maintaining a Hold rating for now.

data.title: FS KKR Capital: Buy The Tariff Tantrum, Secure A 13.7% Yield
data.description:
Improving credit quality (a lower non-accrual ratio) was the main reason underpinning my bullish investment thesis on business development company FS KKR Capital Corp. (NYSE:FSK) at the start of the year.

The BDC’s performance, however, has taken a backseat to the tariff tantrum that has made a lot of business development companies complete bargains.

With the stock of FS KKR Capital now selling again for a rather substantial (more than 20%) discount to net asset value, I think FS KKR Capital is a value stock waiting to get scooped up.

Shrinking Portfolio, Credit Profile And Deteriorating NII-Based Coverage

On the surface, FS KKR Capital doesn’t look to be a prime target for passive income investors: The business development company has a higher-than-normal non-accrual ratio (which also again rose in the December-quarter) and its net investment income fell in 4Q24 thanks to higher loan repayments.

As a consequence, the BDC’s portfolio has shrunk, which triggered a decrease in its net investment income which in turn precipitated an increase in the BDC’s dividend pay-out ratio.

Despite these facts, however, I think that FS KKR Capital is a promising BDC buy for passive income investors, mainly because the company has upside tied to an improving non-accrual ratio.

Most BDCs that I cover have had non-accrual ratios somewhere between 1.0% and 2.0%, so with a 4Q24 non-accrual ratio of 2.2% (based on fair value), FS KKR Capital clearly stands out from the crowd in a negative way.

Compared to FSK’s history, however, the present non-accrual ratio has much improved and it is less than half of the 4Q23 non-accrual ratio. What makes a high non-accrual ratio appealing, ironically, is that BDCs like FSK have the opportunity to improve their credit profiles by renegotiating loan terms or extending maturities.

Another potential point of contention is the BDC’s net investment income trend, which is pointing south. FS KKR Capital’s borrowers repaid an average of $1.5 billion in loans per quarter in 2024 which led to the BDC having negative net investment activity in 3 out of the last 4 quarters.

Since the end of 2023, FS KKR Capital’s portfolio has shrunk a total of 8%, or $1.2 billion, which has triggered a commensurate decrease in the BDC’s net investment income.

FS KKR Capital’s net investment income in 4Q24 amounted to $171 million, down 14.5% YoY thanks to a decrease primarily in interest income which is the consequence of a falling principal balance. FS KKR Capital’s total interest income in the fourth quarter was $257 million, down 22.5% year-over-year.

The decrease in interest income was partially compensated for by higher payment-in-kind interest income as well as other income. The fall in net investment income is the main reason why FS KKR Capital’s dividend pay-out metrics deteriorated in 4Q24.

A shrinking investment portfolio that produces a lower amount of recurring net investment income obviously poses pay-out challenges and FS KKR Capital experienced such a challenge in the fourth quarter: The BDC’s dividend pay-out ratio for the first time in a year rose to more than 100%.

While the LTM dividend pay-out ratio remained below 100% in 2024, the BDC could potentially run into a dividend coverage challenge in the short-term.

On the flip side, since FS KKR Capital pays a base $0.64 per share per quarter dividend that is supplemented by a variable $0.06 per share per quarter dividend, the BDC could scrap or suspend its supplement in order to relieve pressure on its net investment income.

Because of the drop in net investment income that occurred in the fourth quarter thanks to higher loan repayments and high non-accruals, FS KKR Capital has fallen to the second-lowest rank in the BDC peer group in terms of LTM dividend pay-out ratio.

The LTM dividend pay-out ratio, in my view, is the single most important BDC comparison metric. In the last twelve months, FS KKR Capital paid out 98% of its net investment income, and only Golub Capital BDC Inc. (GBDC) had weaker dividend coverage. The peer group average LTM dividend pay-out ratio is 88%.

Market Plunge Opens Up Buying Window At 0.79x NAV

FS KKR Capital is now selling, once again, for a very much exaggerated discount to net asset value which even under the consideration of the higher-than-average non-accrual ratio doesn’t make much sense.

FS KKR Capital is selling for a 21% discount to net asset value (which stood at $23.64 as of December 31, 2024) which makes the business development company the BDC with the fourth-lowest Price-to-NAV ratio, after Prospect Capital Corp. (PSEC), New Mountain Finance Corp. (NMFC) and Goldman Sachs BDC Inc. (GSBD).

The two best BDCs, in my view, are Ares Capital Corp. (ARCC) and Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL), both of which convince with their credit profiles, particularly BXSL, and with their dividend pay-out consistency over a long stretch of time.

FS KKR Capital could be a compelling passive income vehicle if the business development company manages to gain control of its non-accruals and successively improves its credit profile and by doing so also catalyzed a rebound in its net investment income.

In this particular case, I would not be surprised to see a stock re-rating to net asset value which kind of gives the BDC 26% upside to the implied intrinsic value, in addition to the 13.7% dividend yield.

Why I Might Be Wrong About FS KKR Capital

If the business development company were to experience an ongoing uptrend in its non-accrual ratio or its portfolio were to continue to shrink, then FS KKR Capital has substantial dividend risk as the BDC would almost certainly under-earn its dividend with net investment income.

Since the U.S. economy seems to still be in great shape at the moment, despite the tariff tantrum, I think that FS KKR Capital has more upside than downside which leads to a skewed risk/reward relationship in favor of FSK.

My Conclusion

FS KKR Capital is a better BDC investment choice than the valuation suggests, in my view, but there are certainly some risks that passive income investors should be cognizant of:

  1. FS KKR Capital’s net investment income has been on the decline due to higher repayments in 2024;
  2. The BDC has higher-than-average non-accruals; and
  3. FS KKR Capital did not fully cover its dividend with net investment income in 4Q24.

Counterbalancing these risks are an exaggerated discount to net asset value, an overall improving credit profile (compared to 2023), and the fact that the BDC could suspend its supplemental dividend, creating some breathing room for BDC to defend its $0.64 per share per quarter base dividend. With the U.S. economy still growing and interest rates possibly falling further this year, the lending activity for BDC’s should be positive in 2025.

In my view, the fear-driven market correction opens up a buying window even for business development companies that might not immediately pop up on investors’ radars, like FS KKR Capital. That said, though, FS KKR Capital’s stock is promising and with its credit profile potentially improving in 2025, the BDC actually has a catalyst for a stock re-rating.
